data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Nvidia's Intel Deal Boosts Sentiment
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

U.K. retail sales, public sector finances; France monthly business survey; no major corporate trading updates expected

Opening Call:

European stock futures traded slightly higher early Friday. Asian stock benchmarks were mostly higher; the dollar was flat; Treasury yields were up; while oil futures fell and gold inched higher.

Equities:

Stock futures point to a higher open in Europe on Friday, after Nvidia's deal to invest $5 billion in Intel added fuel to a rally on Wall Street following the Federal Reserve's move Wednesday to cut interest rates

The Nasdaq Composite Index logged its 27th closing high of 2025, with the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average also hitting new highs. All three indexes were driven by technology shares, the best-performing sector of the S&P. "We're getting a little bit of adjustment on rates and we're seeing this AI train just full speed ahead," said Marta Norton, chief investment strategist at wealth manager Empower.

"The market is landing on that it's not likely a one-off cut," said Michael Antonelli, a managing director at Baird. Interest-rate futures showed that traders see an 80% chance of a half-point rate cut by the end of the year, according to CME Group data, up from 75% Thursday morning.

Forex:

The U.S. economy and financial markets are showing some resilience and potentially U.S. exceptionalism again, Maybank analysts said.

Markets have reassessed the Fed's stance, with traders taking a more positive view of the FOMC's decision and guidance, NAB's Rodrigo Catril said. Fed Chair Powell's "meeting by meeting" guidance is perceived as prudent, the senior FX strategist said.

Bonds:

Treasury yields continued to rise, with investors in the bond market adjusting their positions following the Fed's shift in monetary policy.

Although the Fed's decision to lower its policy rate by a quarter-percentage point was widely expected, there had been some "creeping hope" in the market for a larger "surprise" cut of a half-percentage point, said Russ Brownback, deputy chief investment officer of fixed income at BlackRock.

Also, the Fed's messaging "could have signaled a bit more of a dovish-forward view," considering the labor market has weakened and tariffs don't appear to be creating "broader knock-on effects on inflation," he said.

Energy:

Oil futures fell, as the high-demand summer season begins to wind down and market participants wait to see what, if any, additional sanctions are placed on Russian oil.

The big decline in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA is attributed mostly to a jump in exports. "While the drawdown in crude inventories was impressive, it seems to be mostly export driven with the help of near multiyear lows in the U.S. dollar, and not necessarily from domestic demand," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial said.

Metals:

Gold edged higher in Asia amid a cautious mood. The market still sees a high probability of another 25-basis-point rate cut by the Fed in October, but the central bank's guidance leaned slightly hawkish, Sky Links Capital Group's Daniel Takieddine said.

Fed officials seem reluctant to fully endorse deeper easing, with the FOMC meeting's outcome leaving the precious metal sensitive to coming economic data, the CEO added.

-

Copper rose in Asian trading. The bull market in precious metals could broaden and shift into base metals in 2026, supported by the prospect of new dovish Fed leadership, lower U.S. rates and downward pressure on the dollar, Citi analysts said.

-

Iron ore prices strengthened amid solid demand, Baocheng Futures analysts said. Chinese steel mills are replenishing stocks before the coming holidays, coinciding with a slight rebound in terminal iron-ore consumption that has also propped up prices, they said.

However, the upward momentum could be limited. While overseas shipments are increasing substantially, China's domestic iron ore supply is recovering as well. Iron ore prices will likely fluctuate at high levels in tandem with changes in supply and demand, they added.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Trump's Team Explores Government-Backed Manufacturing Boost

WASHINGTON-President Trump's team is weighing a plan to spur the construction of factories and other infrastructure in a bid to jump-start the American manufacturing sector, according to documents and people familiar with the discussions.

Under the plan, the administration would use money from a $550 billion investment fund established as part of trade negotiations with Japan to invest in the development of semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, critical minerals, energy, ships and quantum computing.

U.K. Consumer Sentiment Falls Back

The mood of British consumers dampened a little this month, with expectations of a slowing economy and the potential for tax increases later this year threatening an even greater downturn.

The headline level of consumer confidence fell back by two points to minus 19 in September, according to research group GfK's monthly index, published with the Nuremberg Institute for Market Decisions on Friday.

Europe's EV Revolution Is on Track. How Auto Makers Plan to Win.

While Donald Trump's U.S. slams the brakes on the electric-vehicle transition, the European Union is keeping its foot to the floor, probably.

An EU statute passed in 2022 will ban the sale of new gasoline-powered internal-combustion passenger cars by 2035.

Protesters in France Push Back on Efforts to Rein In Spending

PARIS-Hundreds of thousands of protesters poured into the streets across France on Thursday, piling pressure on President Emmanuel Macron and his newly appointed prime minister to abandon any austerity measures aimed at repairing the country's depleted public finances.

A day of mostly peaceful protests turned violent as night fell and protesters clashed with police in Place de la Nation in Paris. Protesters hurled projectiles at columns of police. Officers clad in riot gear released tear gas before charging at crowds that refused to disperse.

Jensen Huang Walks an AI Tightrope Between the U.S. and China

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang is trying to keep both the U.S. and China happy. It is proving to be a tricky high-wire act.

The chip maker drew praise from President Trump for its $5 billion investment in rival Intel, which recently struck a deal to give a 10% stake to the U.S. government. Huang was beaming on a joint video call with Intel Chief Executive Lip-Bu Tan as he discussed a new partnership to make processors for data centers and personal computers.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

06:00/GER: Aug PPI

06:00/UK: Aug Public sector finances

06:00/UK: Aug UK monthly retail sales figures

06:45/FRA: Sep Monthly business survey (goods-producing industries)

07:00/SPN: Jul Industrial Orders & Turnover

08:00/ICE: Aug Harmonized CPI

08:30/UK: 2Q Bank of England statistics on UK banks' external claims

08:30/UK: Aug Monthly Insolvency statistics

15:59/GRE: Jul Balance of Payments

16:59/LUX: 2Q Balance of Payments

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 19, 2025 00:19 ET (04:19 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 12 AM ET: Exxon Sparks Investor Rights Battle. Why It Could Spread. | Oil ...
data.description:
Exxon Sparks Investor Rights Battle. Why It Could Spread.

The oil giant has unveiled a system to let investors automatically support management proposals. Activists are alarmed.

----

Oil Futures Post Back-to-Back Losses

Oil futures added to yesterday's losses as the high-demand summer season began to wind down and the market waited to see what if any additional sanctions were placed on Russian oil.

----

U.S. Natural Gas Falls on Above-Estimate Storage Build

U.S. natural gas futures fell after the EIA reported an above-consensus 90 billion-cubic-foot increase in underground storage, extending the inventory surplus over the five-year average to more than 200 Bcf.

----

California Wants to Halt Oil Industry Exodus After Years of Climate Focus

Policymakers are trying to stave off a potential fuel-supply crunch while refineries look to close.

----

Exxon Mobil to Halt European Recycling Plant Investments

The company is halting more than $100 million in planned investments for two European chemical recycling projects because of concerns over new rules that the company says will undermine the projects' business case.

----

Santos Confirms XRG Consortium Pulls $18.7 Billion Takeover Bid

Santos confirmed that a consortium led by Abu Dhabi National Oil Co. has abandoned a $18.7 billion takeover offer, highlighting disagreements over terms given the length of time it could take for a deal to complete.

----

Mexican Government Issues Debt to Finance Pemex Buybacks

The Mexican government said it issued $13.8 billion in debt in U.S. dollars and euros as part of a series of transactions aimed at capitalizing and shoring up the finances of state oil company Petróleos Mexicanos.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Santos and oil futures in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Post Large Decline

Crude oil inventories fell by 9.3 million barrels to 415.4 million barrels in the week ended Sept. 12 as exports almost doubled and imports fell.

----

Beleaguered Offshore Wind Gets Two Small Signs of Hope

The Department of Justice filed a legal brief earlier this month supporting Empire Wind, an offshore wind project near New York's Long Island that the administration had previously halted.

----

Oil, Gas Investment Needed to Keep Output Steady as Field Declines Accelerate, IEA Says

The world must invest an average of$540 billion through 2050 in the upstream sector or face a reduced global production of 5.5 million barrels a day for the next decade, a new report stated.

----

Energy Department Awards BWX $1.5 Billion Contract

A BWX Technologies unit was awarded a National Nuclear Security Administration contract for work on a Domestic Uranium Enrichment Centrifuge Experiment pilot plant. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 19, 2025 00:15 ET (04:15 GMT)

data.title: Huawei Unveils Fully Upgraded AI-Centric Xinghe Intelligent Network, Accelerating Intelligent Transformation Across Industries
data.description:
Huawei Unveils Fully Upgraded AI-Centric Xinghe Intelligent Network, Accelerating Intelligent Transformation Across Industries

Huawei Unveils Fully Upgraded AI-Centric Xinghe Intelligent Network, Accelerating Intelligent Transformation Across Industries

PR Newswire

SHANGHAI, Sept. 18, 2025

SHANGHAI, Sept. 18, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- During HUAWEI CONNECT 2025, Huawei hosted the data communication innovation summit themed "Xinghe Intelligent Network: Shaping Intelligent Connectivity in the AI Era." At the event, Leon Wang, President of Huawei's Data Communication Product Line, announced the fully upgraded AI-centric Xinghe Intelligent Network Solution. This solution features a three-layer architecture—AI-centric brain, AI-centric connectivity, and AI-centric devices—to accelerate the deep integration of AI and networks. It provides enterprises with diverse scenario-specific connectivity services and experiences, such as zero packet loss, high throughput, deterministic low latency, and all-scenario zero-trust security. Guided by the philosophy of AI-ready networks, AI-powered networks, and AI-powered security, this solution unlocks all intelligence across industries.

AI is reshaping the world at unprecedented speed, transforming enterprise operations and posing three major demands for networks: efficient computing power release, assured service experience, and protection against unknown threats. In response, Huawei's Xinghe Intelligent Network adopts an AI-centric three-layer architecture to fully empower four core solutions: Xinghe AI Campus, Xinghe Intelligent WAN, Xinghe AI Fabric 2.0, and Xinghe AI Network Security—turbocharging intelligent transformation across industries.

  • Xinghe AI Campus extends security from the digital world to the physical world, building a campus with full-scope security. Specifically, Huawei's Wi-Fi Shield and unauthorized access prevention technologies launched last year effectively address air interface and endpoint access security. To counter hidden camera threats, Huawei introduces the industry's first spycam-detecting wireless access point (AP), capable of accurately identifying hidden cameras and protecting trade secrets and privacy 24/7. In executive offices, R&D labs, and other critical areas, Wi-Fi sensing enables centimeter-level micro-motion detection, accurately identifying human presence and ensuring seamless, full-coverage spatial security.
  • Xinghe AI Fabric 2.0 provides intelligent assurance for general-purpose computing and AI computing, fully unleashing computing power. In general-purpose computing scenarios, unified simulation of switches and security devices shortens new service rollouts from 7 days to 5 minutes. A single device supports real-time sensing of over 200,000 service flows, enabling detection of any faults in seconds. In AI computing scenarios, Huawei's in-house AI sensing and unified training-inference scheduling engine enables agile switching between training and inference roles of GPUs, achieving 100% compute utilization and a 10% boost in inference performance. Huawei also introduces a unique four-plane architecture with two-layer cluster networking to support 100,000 GPUs, reducing costs by 40% compared to traditional three-layer designs and building an optimal AI computing foundation.
  • Xinghe Intelligent WAN enables high-capacity, integrated computing-network services, delivering on-demand computing power for enterprises. It offers intelligent enterprise private line services in two editions (Basic and Pro), and leverages the lossless Starnet algorithm and Starnet vector engine to achieve zero-packet-loss transmission over long distances and computing efficiency loss below 5% while keeping sensitive data on-premises. This solution significantly reduces initial investment, supports elastic scaling, and ensures both data security and efficient compute utilization.
  • Xinghe AI Network Security centers on "AI vs. AI" to build an all-scenario AI zero-trust security foundation. Huawei has trained comprehensive AI security models using massive amounts of data from over 100 cloud nodes around the globe. This helps achieve a 95% detection rate for unknown threats—an industry-leading figure. With Huawei's in-house AI Core (dedicated computing power), these AI security models are integrated into local firewalls. This capability, combined with the Emulator engine for unpacking virus variants, identifies and blocks threats in real time.

Additionally, Huawei NetMaster, a network agent that enables 24/7 autonomous operations and maintenance (O&M) through AI, ensures zero network interruptions and reliable connectivity. It accurately senses AP deployment locations, environmental interference, and terminal experience. Upon detecting typical Wi-Fi issues such as weak coverage or high interference, it calculates and executes optimal solutions across coverage, interference, and load dimensions. In this way, 80% of wireless faults can be automatically resolved.

At the summit, Huawei also unveiled joint innovation achievements with top industry customers including Tsinghua University, Peking University, Shandong University, and iFLYTEK, and announced global showcases with industry pioneers like Shenzhen Power Supply Bureau, Shenzhen Welkin School, China Pacific Insurance (Group) Co., Ltd., and Resorts World Sentosa (Singapore). These benchmark cases span education, electric power, finance, and key enterprise sectors, setting an example for global digital and intelligent transformation.

Adhering to the philosophy of "AI for All, All on IP", Huawei will continue to innovate and upgrade its Xinghe Intelligent Network products and solutions, and collaborate with global customers and partners to build AI-centric networks and jointly shape the intelligent era.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/huawei-unveils-fully-upgraded-ai-centric-xinghe-intelligent-network-accelerating-intelligent-transformation-across-industries-302561263.html

SOURCE Huawei

data.title: Health Canada approves Celltrion’s Stoboclo® and Osenvelt®(CT-P41), denosumab biosimilars for the treatment of all indications approved for the reference products, Prolia® and Xgeva®, respectively
data.description:
Health Canada approves Celltrion’s Stoboclo® and Osenvelt®(CT-P41), denosumab biosimilars for the treatment of all indications approved for the reference products, Prolia® and Xgeva®, respectively

  • Stoboclo® and Osenvelt® (CT-P41) received Health Canada Notice of Compliance for all indications referencing Prolia® and Xgeva®, respectively1,2
  • The approval is based on the totality of evidence including results from a Phase III clinical trial in postmenopausal women with osteoporosis (PMO)3
  • Celltrion’s biosimilar portfolio continues to grow, expanding treatment options to reach more patients in Canada 

Celltrion today announced that Health Canada has approved Stoboclo® and Osenvelt® (CT-P41), biosimilars referencing Prolia® and Xgeva®, respectively for all indications of reference products.1,2

“The approval of Stoboclo and Osenvelt underscores our commitment to developing patient-centric therapies, particularly for incurable forms of disease such as osteoporosis where patients face difficult prognoses,” said Jungyong Shin, Managing Director at Celltrion Healthcare Canada. “We are proud to lead the biosimilar industry with our expanded portfolio, offering significant cost-saving potential while enhancing patient access and supporting the Canadian skeletal disorders community.”

The Health Canada approval is based on robust clinical evidence, including results from a Phase III clinical trial in postmenopausal women with osteoporosis designed to evaluate the efficacy, pharmacodynamics (PD), pharmacokinetics (PK), safety and immunogenicity of CT-P41 to reference denosumab. Study results demonstrated that CT-P41 had equivalent efficacy and pharmacodynamics to reference denosumab with similar pharmacokinetics and comparable safety and immunogenicity profiles.3

Stoboclo and Osenvelt are Celltrion’s sixth biosimilars approved in Canada after receiving the company’s approval of Remdantry™/ Remsima® SC (infliximab), Yuflyma® (adalimumab), Vegzelma® (bevacizumab), SteQeyma® (ustekinumab), Omlyclo® (omalizumab).

Notes to Editors:

About Stoboclo®

Stoboclo® is a receptor activator of NF-κb ligand (RANKL) inhibitor (bone metabolism regulator) developed as a biosimilar referencing Prolia® (denosumab) approved in Canada. Stoboclo has been approved for indications approved for the reference products, Prolia. Denosumab biosimilars and reference product (Prolia) have been approved in Canada to treat postmenopausal women with osteoporosis at high risk for fracture, to increase bone mass in men with osteoporosis at high risk for fracture, to increase bone mass in men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer receiving androgen deprivation therapy (ADT), to increase bone mass in women with nonmetastatic breast cancer receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor (AI) therapy, and to increase bone mass for the treatment and prevention of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis in women and men at high risk for fracture. Stoboclo is available as 60 mg / mL solution for injection in prefilled syringe.1

About Osenvelt®

Osenvelt® is a receptor activator of NF-κb ligand (RANKL) inhibitor (bone metabolism regulator) developed as a biosimilar referencing Xgeva® (denosumab) approved in Canada. Osenvelt has been approved for indications approved for the reference products, Xgeva. Denosumab biosimilars and reference product (Xgeva) are indicated to reduce the risk of developing skeletal-related events in patients with multiple myeloma and in patients with bone metastases from breast cancer, prostate cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, and other solid tumours, to treat adults and skeletally mature adolescents with giant cell tumour of bone that is unresectable or where surgical resection is likely to result in severe morbidity, and to treat hypercalcemia of malignancy that is refractory to intravenous bisphosphonate. Osenvelt is available as 120 mg/1.7 mL solution for injection in single-use vial.2

About Celltrion Inc.

Celltrion is a leading biopharmaceutical company that specialises in researching, developing, manufacturing, marketing and sales of innovative therapeutics that improve people's lives worldwide. Celltrion is a pioneer in the biosimilar space, having launched the world's first monoclonal antibody biosimilar. Our global pharmaceutical portfolio addresses a range of therapeutic areas including immunology, oncology, haematology, ophthalmology and endocrinology. Beyond biosimilar products, we are committed to advancing our pipeline with novel drugs to push the boundaries of scientific innovation and deliver quality medicines. For more information, please visit our website www.celltrion.com/en-us and stay updated with our latest news and events on our social media - LinkedIn, Instagram, X, and Facebook.

About Celltrion Healthcare Canada Limited

Celltrion Healthcare Canada is committed to delivering innovative and affordable medications to promote patients’ access to advanced therapies. Its products are manufactured at state-of-the-art mammalian cell culture facilities, designed and built to comply with Health Canada regulations. With the approval of Stoboclo® and Osenvelt®, Celltrion now has eight products across six biosimilars approved by Health Canada: Remdantry™/ Remsima® SC (infliximab), Yuflyma® (adalimumab), Vegzelma® (bevacizumab), SteQeyma® (ustekinumab), Omlyclo® (omalizumab), Stoboclo® and Osenvelt® (denosumab). For more information, please visit: https://www.celltrionhealthcare.ca

Stoboclo® and Osenvelt® are trademarks of Celltrion, Inc. and are used under license.
Prolia® and Xgeva® are registered trademarks of Amgen Inc.

____________________

1 Stoboclo Product Monograph. Celltrion Inc. September 2025. Product information from Health Canada.
2 Osenvelt Product Monograph. Celltrion Inc. September 2025. Product information from Health Canada.
3 Reginster JY et al. Efficacy and safety of candidate biosimilar CT-P41 versus reference denosumab: a double-blind, randomized, active-controlled, Phase 3 trial in postmenopausal women with osteoporosis. Osteoporos Int. 2024 Nov;35(11):1919-1930. doi: 10.1007/s00198-024-07161-x. Epub 2024 Jul 23. PMID: 39042292; PMCID: PMC11499533. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39042292/ [Last accessed September 2025].

Media Relations Contact
info_CA@celltrionhc.com
Jeeyoung.kim@celltrionhc.com

data.title: Why Choose Trice Contracting for Your Concrete Needs? Concrete Services Queens New York
data.description:
Why Choose Trice Contracting for Your Concrete Needs? Concrete Services Queens New York

Why Choose Trice Contracting for Your Concrete Needs? Concrete Services Queens New York

PR Newswire

QUEENS, N.Y., Sept. 15, 2025

QUEENS, N.Y., Sept. 15, 2025 /PRNewswire/ --A Story from the Heart of Queens, New York

In the heart of Queens, New York, where tradition meets innovation and every square foot of property matters, one name continues to rise above the rest in the world of concrete services - Trice Contracting.

This past weekend, Trice Contracting hosted a hands-on, community-centered event designed to educate, inspire, and connect with New Yorkers ready to invest in their homes and businesses. The theme?

"Why Choose Trice Contracting for Your Concrete Needs?"

As it turns out, the answer was written in every poured slab, every detailed finish, and every handshake from the Trice team.

A Community Comes Together

Set in a bustling corner of Queens, the event drew in local homeowners, commercial property owners, and even a few real estate investors - all curious to learn what sets Trice apart. What they found wasn't just a contracting company, but a team of craftsmen passionate about their work and committed to their clients.

Attendees walked through live concrete demonstrations, where plain surfaces were transformed into eye-catching, durable finishes — including stamped patios, colored walkways, polished floors, and weather-resistant driveways.

One guest, a longtime Queens resident, stood quietly watching a demo before saying, "I've hired contractors before. But this? This is a whole different level of care and detail."

What Makes Trice Contracting Different?

At the heart of it all is the Trice philosophy:

  • Do the job right.
  • Communicate every step.
  • Treat every project like it's your own home.

This wasn't just a sales event - it was a demonstration of values. Visitors got one-on-one time with project managers, browsed portfolios of past work in Queens and the greater NYC area, and asked real questions about timelines, budgets, and design options.

More than that, they got transparency.

While other contractors might upsell or overpromise, Trice's team emphasized realistic planning, long-term durability, and affordable customization. Their knowledge of local building codes, weather patterns, and borough-specific permits made them an obvious choice for serious property owners.

It's More Than Concrete - It's Your Foundation

Concrete may seem like just a material, but for Trice Contracting, it's the foundation of a dream - a safer driveway for your kids to play on, a patio for your family to gather, a solid walkway welcoming guests to your business.

By the end of the event, many visitors walked away not just with brochures and estimates — but with real plans, booked consultations, and a clear vision of how their property could be elevated.

Looking Ahead

As Trice Contracting continues to expand its footprint in Queens and across New York City, events like this serve as a reminder:

Quality work, personal service, and real community connection never go out of style.

If you missed the event, don't worry — the Trice team is ready to meet with you anytime.

Call Now for a Free Estimate: 718-278-6000

Trice Contracting – Built on Trust. Poured with Precision. Concrete Services in Queens New York – providing services in all 5 boroughs of New York.

For more information about Trice Contracting Inc. services, visit www.tricecontracting.com or contact their office at 718-278-6000.

data.title: Google searches for 'help with mortgage' haven't been this high since 2009
data.description:
By Aarthi Swaminathan

The uptick in interest in mortgage help has raised fears that another foreclosure crisis is looming

Online searches for "help with mortgage" have increased, which some see as a signal that the U.S. economy is slowing.

A chart circulating on social media that shows a sudden uptick in online searches for the phrase "help with mortgage" is raising concerns that we could see a repeat of the 2008 foreclosure crisis.

But the reality isn't nearly as dire, economists say.

The chart was first highlighted by X users, including the betting market Polymarket, which added the ominous warning: "BREAKING: Searches for "help with mortgage" surpass 2008 housing crisis."

While it's true that search interest in the phrase "help with mortgage" recently shot up, the actual previous peak for the phrase was in 2009, not 2008, according to Google Trends.

Google Trends doesn't show exact volumes of searches; it estimates how popular a search term has been since 2004, when the company went public.

Before now, the last big surge in search interest for "help with mortgage" was in March 2020, right at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic. But that jump did not exceed 2009 levels, according to Google Trends.

The viral chart seemed to set off alarm bells for some.

It's important to note that these search trends only represent what people appear to be looking for on the internet, rather than the actual number of homeowners in distress.

In other words, the people googling "help with mortgage" may be financially stable, but could be pre-emptively looking up mortgage assistance because they fear being laid off, for instance. And the phrase "help with mortgage" could also refer to people wanting help obtaining a mortgage.

Additionally, recent search traffic may not be directly comparable to 2009, when internet adoption was less widespread.

"In general, I would be wary of drawing strong conclusions from Google trends data. Methodologies and search patterns change a lot, so it's hard to compare over time, especially so far back," Jake Krimmel, senior economist at Realtor.com, told MarketWatch.

(Realtor.com is operated by News Corp subsidiary Move Inc.; MarketWatch publisher Dow Jones is also a subsidiary of News Corp.)

But at the same time, other Google search trends and mortgage-delinquency data illustrate growing distress among homeowners.

Consider the related searches that have gained interest over the past three months, according to Google Trends. They indicate that people are increasingly looking for charities that help with mortgage payments, mortgage-assistance grants and government help with their mortgages, to name a few.

The searches also come at a time when people are missing their mortgage payments, according to housing-industry data.

Mortgage delinquencies have been broadly rising over the past few months, MarketWatch has reported previously. Serious delinquencies, which refer to loans that are over 90 days past due, were up 30,000 in July as compared with a year ago, according to data from Intercontinental Exchange.

First-time and lower-income homeowners in particular are struggling to pay their mortgages. ICE noted that the "primary driver of stress" in the mortgage market was among mortgages backed by the Federal Housing Administration, typically used by first-time buyers or lower-income people. FHA loans accounted for 52% of serious delinquencies nationwide in July, the company noted.

FHA loans are more flexible than other mortgages. Borrowers can have a credit score as low as 580 and a down payment of only 3.5%, while typical mortgages require higher scores and higher down payment amounts.

"The data have shown for the last year or two that lower-income households are struggling with their finances much more so than the rest of families. This has likely driven an uptick in delinquencies for auto loans and credit cards. The same dynamic shows itself in the data on mortgage delinquencies," Stephen Stanley, chief U.S. economist at Santander, told MarketWatch.

But the delinquency rate on conventional mortgages, which most homeowners take on, has been far lower and more stable than the delinquency rates for FHA and Department of Veterans Affairs loans.

Borrowers with FHA loans were struggling more than homeowners with other types of loans, such as conventional mortgages.

For that reason, even though there is some evidence of rising distress among homeowners today, it's not ringing alarm bells just yet, experts told MarketWatch.

"It can be true at the same time that the overall picture is largely favorable but that there is a rising trend of payment difficulty for the most vulnerable homeowners. I suspect that the search results reflect this," Stanley added.

Read more: More people are late on both their mortgage and student-loan payments - another bad sign for the economy

Overall, foreclosures are rising broadly, but still remain below prepandemic levels.

The number of properties in the U.S. associated with a foreclosure filing, which refers to default notices, scheduled auctions or bank repossessions, was up 18.1% in August compared with the same month a year ago, according to a report by property-data provider Attom. In other words, about 1 in 4,000 housing units had a foreclosure filing associated with it.

That number has been rising for the past six months. But again, it's not yet at alarming levels.

Taking a step back, Realtor.com's Krimmel said that the internet's theory that the housing market may be facing mass foreclosures like it did during the 2007-'09 financial crisis didn't hold - for now.

If one were to look for cracks in the housing market, "I would pay much more attention to signals of local labor-market weakness," he said, such as rising unemployment "in areas where house prices are also falling AND where there were a lot of purchases post-2022 rate hikes," he explained.

"For the 'doomer' theories of the 2008 redux to be true, you would need that sort of perfect storm: layoffs [and] falling house prices [and] high share of recent home purchases," he added. "The good news is these perfect storms seem to be few and geographically isolated."

Related: Home prices grow at slowest pace in two years, reflecting 'extraordinarily weak' demand

And so even if delinquencies and foreclosures are on the rise broadly, "they're also not a sign of a housing or financial crisis right now either," Krimmel said, as other parts of the economy are still doing fine at the moment.

-Aarthi Swaminathan

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-18-25 2250ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Travel Answers Group Welcomes Jonna Jackson as Director - Europe, Leading Strategic Expansion into Europe
data.description:
Travel Answers Group Welcomes Jonna Jackson as Director - Europe, Leading Strategic Expansion into Europe

Travel Answers Group Welcomes Jonna Jackson as Director - Europe, Leading Strategic Expansion into Europe

PR Newswire

BELLEVUE, Wash., Sept. 18, 2025

BELLEVUE, Wash., Sept. 18, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Travel Answers Group, Travel Answers, a leading provider of independent vacation experiences, proudly announces Jonna Jackson as its new Director – Europe.  This marks a significant milestone as Travel Answers launches its curated Europe travel portfolio. Jackson's seasoned expertise, spanning over 25 years in luxury travel with Classic Vacations and deep experience as a luxury travel advisor, positions her to drive Travel Answers' accelerated growth in European destinations.

"We are delighted to welcome Jonna Jackson, whose impressive background in European travel, product development, and advisor support will inspire confidence among travel advisors nationwide," said Kirk Demeter, Founder & CEO of Travel Answers Group. "Her leadership is key to our mission of empowering advisors to deliver tailor-made European journeys that delight clients."

Expertise-Powered Europe Debut
Travel Answers Europe program launches with signature itineraries in Italy, France, Spain, Portugal, and the UK (London), featuring a blend of ready-to-book packages, iconic cultural experiences, and expertly curated small-group tours. Notable among these is the new "Italy Highlights" 9-night journey, with scheduled departures in November & December 2025, with more dates planned in 2026.

Leveraging its proven operational excellence, trusted supplier relationships, and exclusive access through Nexus DMC, Travel Answers ensures seamless trip delivery across Europe. In-country teams in Italy, Greece, Portugal, Austria, and additional markets facilitate high-quality service for every itinerary.

Your Strategic Partner in Selling Europe
As a preferred travel company with top U.S. consortia, Travel Answers reinforces its commitment to advisor success through targeted training, robust marketing support, and dedicated European destination specialists. The company's advisor-first approach empowers agents to confidently sell Europe, supported by exclusive resources, direct access to experts, and a portfolio tailored for authenticity and value.

Jonna Jackson commented, "I'm honored to guide Travel Answers expansion into Europe, partnering with advisors to deliver journeys that balance authenticity and quality. Together, we'll unlock new opportunities for travelers to experience the depth, culture, and discovery that make Europe timeless."

About Travel Answers
Founded in 1992, Travel Answers Group is a trusted leader in tailor-made travel, known for its deep destination expertise, seamless operations, and unwavering support for travel advisors. Building on decades of success across Oceania, Africa, and Asia, Travel Answers now brings its proven quality and reliability to Europe.

With a team of Destination Specialists who have lived in or extensively explored the regions they represent, and strong partnership with Nexus DMC, Travel Answers offers advisors immersive, authentic European experiences backed by local insight and dedicated on-the-ground support.

Preferred by major U.S. travel consortia, Travel Answers empowers advisors with the knowledge, tools, and confidence to grow their business and deliver exceptional journeys. With over three decades of proven success and Founder & CEO Kirk Demeter's established industry leadership, Travel Answers continues to set the standard for destination excellence and advisor partnership across four continents.

For more details, visit www.travelanswersgroup.com or contact Jonna Jackson - jonna.jackson@travelanswersgroup.com.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/travel-answers-group-welcomes-jonna-jackson-as-director--europe-leading-strategic-expansion-into-europe-302561230.html

SOURCE Travel Answers Group

data.title: Why mortgage rates are actually going up after the Fed cut interest rates
data.description:
By Aarthi Swaminathan

'We actually anticipated the possibility that rates would tick up after the Fed announcement,' economist says

The 30-year mortgage rate shot up the day after the Federal Reserve cut interest rates.

Hours after the Federal Reserve cut its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points on Wednesday, mortgage rates ticked up 9 basis points.

Why did that happen? And will mortgage rates continue to climb from here?

While this trend might seem counterintuitive, the jump in mortgage rates was relatively small and driven by how financial markets are assessing the central bank's next move, economists told MarketWatch.

And it wasn't unprecedented. In fact, MarketWatch wrote about this exact trend occurring last year, after the Fed's jumbo 50 basis-point rate cut in September 2024.

From the archives (September 2024): Mortgage rates went up right after the Fed cut interest rates. Here's why.

The Fed announced Wednesday that it would trim its key policy rate by a quarter of a percentage point, bringing it to the range of 4% to 4.25%. Around the time of the announcement, Mortgage News Daily, a website that posts daily updates on rates, crashed - possibly the result of people flocking to the site to see how mortgage rates reacted. The company told MarketWatch it was looking into why the site was down that afternoon.

Mortgage News Daily later reported that the 30-year rate went up by 9 basis points (0.09%) to 6.22% on Wednesday. On Thursday, it reported that the 30-year rate had gone up by 15 more basis points, to 6.37%.

In contrast, a report by Freddie Mac measuring weekly averages for the 30-year rate found that mortgage rates fell to the lowest level in 12 months on Thursday. That's because Freddie Mac's report gathered information prior to and after the Fed's decision was announced. The weekly report doesn't survey lenders, but is based on actual mortgage applications to lenders across the country that are sent to Freddie Mac.

Why did mortgage rates go up hours after the Fed cut rates?

"We actually anticipated the possibility that rates would tick up after the Fed announcement," Danielle Hale, chief economist at Realtor.com, told MarketWatch. "This has to do with the market's expectations for policy moving forward." (Realtor.com is operated by News Corp subsidiary Move Inc.; MarketWatch publisher Dow Jones is also a subsidiary of News Corp.)

Mortgage rates aren't tied to the Fed's interest-rate moves. Instead, they typically fall in advance of a Fed rate cut, as MarketWatch has reported, because bond investors are trying to anticipate where the central bank will go. Mortgage rates are priced off the 10-year Treasury note BX:TMUBMUSD10Y by adding a spread.

Hence, the 10-year Treasury yield is a better gauge of how mortgage rates will move - and the 10-year yield was trending higher Thursday.

Read more: Fed cuts rates - and the 10-year Treasury yield climbs

"Investors are focused on where policy and rates will go in the future - not just today - when they are investing for the longer term," Hale said.

What's more, "a 25 basis-point rate cut was widely expected, and a small minority of investors were even holding out hope that we would see a larger 50 basis-point cut at this meeting," she added.

Related: Will the Fed's quarter-point cut really help your finances? What it means for mortgages, credit-card bills, savings rates and more.

Where the 30-year mortgage rate goes from here was not immediately clear to the economists who spoke with MarketWatch. A mid-August forecast by housing-finance giant Fannie Mae expected the 30-year rate to average 6.5% in the fourth quarter of this year, and fall to 6.1% by the third quarter of next year.

The Fed also signaled this week that it would cut rates two more times this year. Investors are adjusting to the Fed's potential rate-cutting plans, Hale noted, so mortgage rates could stay in the current range for the time being.

The 10-year Treasury yield moving higher on Wednesday after the rate cut "suggests that investors may have realized that their expectations are a little ahead of the Fed's," she said, "and we may not see rates ease quite as fast as currently expected."

In other words, "buyers and sellers looking for their next home can expect continued volatility in mortgage rates over the next few weeks," George Ratiu, vice president of research at the National Apartment Association, told MarketWatch.

As rates remain relatively lower now than in months past, some may be wondering whether this is a good time to buy a home or refinance an existing mortgage.

People are still largely holding off on buying homes. Purchase activity, which refers to people applying for a mortgage to buy a house, has been relatively muted in recent weeks despite the big drops in mortgage rates.

But refinances have taken off, as MarketWatch reported previously.

Read more: Homeowners pounce on falling mortgage rates, with more opting for risky, adjustable-rate loans

The most common reason people were refinancing was to lower their monthly mortgage payments, Michael Read, principal at Bridgeway Mortgage and Real Estate Services, told MarketWatch.

"Mortgage rates were in the high 6%, low 7% at different points over the past two years," he said, so refinancing at a rate in the low-6% range helps those homeowners save some money.

-Aarthi Swaminathan

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-18-25 2205ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
